data.title: Park National GAAP EPS of $2.39 misses by $0.21, revenue of $159.5M beats by $2.43M
data.description:
• Park National press release (PRK): Q1 GAAP EPS of $2.39 misses by $0.21.
  • Revenue of $159.5M (+22.6% Y/Y) beats by $2.43M.

data.title: Stocks to watch on Friday after hours: LEE, WABC, MCBS
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages ended mixed on Friday, but the S&P 500 (SP500) and the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) achieved another record close as investors digested the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension to the truce between Israel and Lebanon.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) ended +0.8% reaching the all-time high close of 7,165.08 points, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) closed +1.6%, also at the record close of 24,836.60 points. Meanwhile, the blue-chip Dow was -0.2%.

Now here are the 3 stocks to watch on Friday after hours—usually a quiet session ahead of the weekend:

  • Lee Enterprises (LEE) said it has appointed Nathan Bekke as president and CEO and Josh Rinehults as vice president, CFO and treasurer, with both roles made permanent after serving on an interim basis since February 2026. The stock was little changed at press time.
  • Westamerica Bancorporation (WABC) said its board had approved a 2M share increase to its stock repurchase authorization, leaving 2.79M shares available under the expanded plan, or about 11.8% of shares outstanding as of March 31, 2026. Shares slipped 0.3% in extended trading.
  • MetroCity Bankshares (MCBS) delivered a double beat on Q1 earnings, with GAAP EPS of $0.77 (beating by $0.05) on revenue of $50.8M (beating by $0.2M). Shares ticked down 0.1% in after-hours trading.

data.title: Customers Bancorp reaffirms 2026 net interest income growth of 7%-11% while expanding $30M operational excellence target
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Customers Bancorp (CUBI) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Q1 2026 was another clear demonstration of a model that is firing on all cylinders" (Vice Chairman, President & CEO Samvir Sidhu). Sidhu said the company’s strategy is unchanged following his move to Customers Bancorp CEO on January 1, adding: "the strategy, the culture and the principles that got us here are not changing."

  • Sidhu highlighted balance sheet momentum and mix, including: total deposits grew 16% annualized, total loans grew 15% annualized, and "Total noninterest-bearing balances grew to a record $6.7 billion, driven by our new teams" (Vice Chairman, President & CEO Sidhu).

  • AI and payments were positioned as core strategic priorities. "We are moving aggressively to operationalize AI across Customers Bank" (Vice Chairman, President & CEO Sidhu), and he described cubiX as "one of the largest commercial payments platforms in the country by transaction volume" while stating the platform is being broadened beyond digital assets into mortgage finance and real estate transaction settlement.

  • "In the quarter, we delivered GAAP and core EPS of $1.97 as GAAP and core earnings were materially consistent" (Executive VP & CFO Mark McCollom). McCollom also reported deposit growth to $21.6 billion and loan growth to $17.4 billion, alongside deposit cost improvements.

Outlook

  • "We are reaffirming our full year 2026 management outlook across all key metrics" (Executive VP & CFO McCollom). He reiterated net interest income growth of 7% to 11% over 2025 and kept full-year noninterest expense at $440 million to $460 million, adding that this implies expense growth of 2% to 6% while investing in people and technology.

  • Management kept loan and deposit growth expectations unchanged despite a strong start, with McCollom citing uncertainty: "there's certainly a lot of still geopolitical uncertainty out there in the market" (Executive VP & CFO McCollom), and saying the company is "still sticking to" the full-year targets.

  • On operational efficiency initiatives, McCollom said Phase 1 of the 2026 operational excellence program has been "substantially achieved" and that the company is "increasing our target by an initial $10 million in Phase 2, bringing our total target to $30 million in run rate proceeds" (Executive VP & CFO McCollom).

Financial results

  • McCollom reported profitability and returns: GAAP and core EPS of $1.97, core ROE of 13.1%, and ROA of 1.13%, while also stating, "Core EPS increasing 28% from last year" (Executive VP & CFO McCollom).

  • Net interest income was reported as $191.4 million. McCollom said the sequential decline was driven by "approximately $10 million of accretion income in the fourth quarter" that did not repeat and a lower day count, while also noting a "planned redemption of $110 million of higher cost subordinated debt late in the quarter" that he said "will help our net interest income in the second quarter" (Executive VP & CFO McCollom).

  • Expense control and efficiency were emphasized: "Noninterest expense was $112 million for the quarter" and "Year-over-year, core revenue growth outpaced core expense growth by nearly 2x" with "our core efficiency ratio improved by 300 basis points" (Executive VP & CFO McCollom).

  • Capital and shareholder returns were highlighted with tangible book value per share at $63.54 and a TCE ratio of 8.3%. "We also repurchased about 620,000 shares of our common stock during the quarter at a weighted average price of about $68" (Executive VP & CFO McCollom).

Q&A

  • Michael Pietrini, JPMorgan: asked for cubiX deposits period-end and average, and timing for new vertical contributions; Executive VP & CFO McCollom replied, "Period-end numbers were right around $4 billion, and quarterly average numbers were right around $3.6 billion" and Vice Chairman, President & CEO Sidhu said he expected "about $250 million or so of noninterest-bearing deposit growth related to new verticals in cubiX just in the next 90 days."

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: asked about AI-driven revenue opportunities and how to think about the net interest income bridge if cubiX balances drift; Vice Chairman, President & CEO Sidhu said AI efforts could produce impacts "as early as the end of the year, but definitely into 2027," and Executive VP & CFO McCollom described deposit-account funding lag and reiterated, "we're not going to move on our guide yet for the year for NII."

  • Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler: asked whether conservatism in deploying cubiX deposits has shifted and about banker hiring; Vice Chairman, President & CEO Sidhu said on digital asset deposits, "we'll continue to sort of hold these in cash for the time being" while stating newer verticals are from "traditional industries" where "Those banks deploy the deposits. We're going to do the same."

  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: questioned stickiness/turnover of mortgage and real estate deposits and loan mix; Vice Chairman, President & CEO Sidhu said the deposits are "sticky and long duration," and Executive VP & CFO McCollom addressed lender finance volatility and said, "we've never had a delinquency or a net charge-off" in that business over 10 years.

  • Peter Winter, D.A. Davidson: asked about professional fees/FDIC expense trajectory and the written agreement, and about reserve build; Vice Chairman, President & CEO Sidhu said "we materially completed our work related to the written agreement at the end of last year," and Executive VP & CFO McCollom said the ACL increase was "just a function" of loan growth with monitoring of geopolitical uncertainty.

  • Kyle Gierman, Hovde: asked about multifamily nonperformers and capital priorities; Executive VP & CFO McCollom said one loan was moved to NPA and "The loan is still performing according to its contractual terms," and laid out capital priorities as "first and foremost, it's organic growth" followed by inorganic opportunities and then buybacks.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive with pointed modeling questions on cubiX deposit durability, NII sensitivity, and credit migration; Kelly Motta said, "I'm still recovering from the shock of the AI clone aspect of the call" (Kelly Motta, KBW), while multiple analysts pressed for mix, stickiness, and deployment assumptions.

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, repeatedly citing conservatism and uncertainty. Executive VP & CFO McCollom used cautionary framing around guidance, including "it's still early," while Vice Chairman, President & CEO Sidhu projected confidence on strategy, saying AI is "not an experiment" and that the company intends to be "a model for risk management."

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management’s emphasis shifted from announcing initial 2026 guidance to reaffirming it, while leaning more heavily into execution detail on AI adoption metrics and near-term cubiX diversification; analysts remained focused on deposits, NII/margin mechanics, and the durability of cubiX.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Leadership messaging shifted as Sidhu noted: "January 1 marked my first day as CEO of Customers Bancorp" (Vice Chairman, President & CEO Sidhu), following the prior quarter’s transition framing led by Jay Sidhu in prepared remarks.

  • Guidance posture moved from setting 2026 targets in Q4 to holding them steady in Q1, with McCollom explicitly reaffirming outlook metrics; Q4 highlighted initial 2026 ranges including net interest income of $800 million to $830 million, while Q1 focused on the same growth framework and reiterated net interest income growth of 7% to 11%.

  • Strategic narrative intensified around AI in Q1, including Sidhu’s disclosure: "The prepared remarks you heard on my behalf today were delivered by my AI clone" (Vice Chairman, President & CEO Sidhu), versus Q4’s focus on enablement and productivity gains.

Risks and concerns

  • Management repeatedly cited external uncertainty as a constraint on changing guidance, with McCollom pointing to "geopolitical uncertainty" as a factor in maintaining targets.

  • Deposit composition and deployment remained a recurring concern. Sidhu said digital-asset-related balances will continue to be held in cash "for the time being" (Vice Chairman, President & CEO Sidhu), while framing new verticals as more deployable given traditional operating-account behavior.

  • Credit watch items were addressed directly, including McCollom’s comment that a multifamily loan moved to NPA was still paying and had been marked to collateral value, alongside his note that the company continues to monitor macro risks.

Final takeaway

Customers Bancorp’s management described Q1 2026 as strong across growth, deposit mix, and operating leverage while reaffirming its full-year outlook. The call centered on scaling AI across workflows, expanding cubiX beyond digital assets into mortgage and real estate settlement, and continuing team-driven organic growth, alongside a raised operational excellence target to $30 million in annual run-rate proceeds and continued emphasis on conservative balance sheet and risk management execution.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Chemed signals 2026 EPS of $24-$24.75 as VITAS raises ADC growth outlook to 4.5%-5.5%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Chemed Corporation (CHE) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "VITAS performance during the quarter exceeded even the high end of our expectations," said (CEO, President & Director Kevin McNamara), citing that VITAS "was able to add ADC" while "keeping hospice labor costs lower than budgeted" and "continuing to add cushion to the Medicare Cap position in our Florida combined program."

  • (CEO McNamara) highlighted VITAS operating metrics, including: "Admissions at VITAS during the quarter totaled 19,394" and "Hospital admissions as a percent of total admissions for our Florida combined program was 43.8%"; he added that VITAS "add[ed] over $32.5 million to cap cushion in the Florida combined program" and concluded, "We are more confident than ever that VITAS has put the Florida cap issue of 2025 behind us and has returned to a normalized rate of growth."

  • (CEO McNamara) said Roto-Rooter showed early signs of stabilization: "For the first time since the fourth quarter of 2022, residential plumbing and residential sewer and drain revenue both increased during the quarter," driven by "an increase in total leads of 3.3%" and "Paid leads during the first quarter of 2026 increased 18.7%." He also said the shift required Roto-Rooter "to increase marketing spend by almost $3 million in the quarter compared to the first quarter of 2025."

  • (CEO McNamara) said extreme winter weather created a revenue headwind: "We estimate that these service disruptions resulted in a net loss revenue of between $3 million and $4 million during the quarter." He also announced franchise buybacks: "On March 31, 2026, Roto-Rooter purchased the territory and assets of the franchises operating in San Francisco, California, and Fort Worth, Texas," with "approximately $20.6 million" of combined purchase price; he said the acquisitions are "anticipated to add between $5 million and $5.5 million of revenue for the remainder of 2026" and are "immediately accretive to earnings."

  • "VITAS net revenue was $420 million in the first quarter of 2026," said (Executive VP, Controller, Principal Accounting Officer & CFO Michael Witzeman), and "Adjusted EBITDA, excluding Medicare Cap, totaled $70.8 million" with "Adjusted EBITDA margin in the quarter, excluding Medicare Cap was 16.8%."

  • (President & CEO of VITAS Healthcare Joel Wherley) said VITAS demand and expansion in Florida remained strong: "our average daily census was 22,723" and "we anticipate opening Manatee County in late second quarter or early third quarter," adding, "I believe the opportunity for growth at VITAS has never been better."

Outlook

  • (CFO Witzeman) said Chemed revised full-year 2026 expectations, stating, "we have increased projections for the remainder of '26" and updated VITAS "Full year ADC growth for 2026" to "a range of 4.5% to 5.5%" and VITAS "revenue growth, excluding the impact of the Medicare Cap" to "a revised range of 6.5% to 7.5%."

  • (CFO Witzeman) raised VITAS profitability framing, saying "revised EBITDA margin, excluding the impact of the Medicare Cap, is anticipated to be 18% to 18.5%."

  • (CFO Witzeman) kept Roto-Rooter revenue growth unchanged but reduced margin expectations: "anticipated revenue growth remains unchanged at 3% to 3.5%" and "Estimated adjusted EBITDA margin is lowered slightly to 21.5% to 22.5%," adding, "This is primarily due to elevated marketing costs now expected to persist above our original guidance for the remainder of the year."

  • (CFO Witzeman) lifted full-year adjusted EPS guidance: "earnings per diluted share... is estimated to be in the range of $24 to $24.75" versus prior guidance he described as "between $23.25 and $24.25," and said the updated guide assumes "an effective corporate tax rate... of 24.5%" and "a diluted share count of 13.6 million shares."

Financial results

  • (CFO Witzeman) detailed VITAS revenue drivers and offsets: the $420 million reflected "a 2.2% increase in days of care" and "a geographically weighted average Medicare reimbursement rate increase of approximately 2.6%," while "The acuity mix shift negatively impacted revenue growth 120 basis points" and "Medicare Cap and other contra revenue changes negatively impacted revenue growth by approximately 47 basis points."

  • (CFO Witzeman) quantified cap and pricing metrics: "VITAS accrued $2.4 million in Medicare Cap billing limitation" and "Average revenue per day... was $210.62"; he added, "No Medicare Cap billing limitation was recorded... for the Florida combined program and none is anticipated for the 2026 fiscal period."

  • (CFO Witzeman) said Roto-Rooter profitability reflected marketing pressure: "Adjusted EBITDA... totaled $53.5 million" and "The adjusted EBITDA margin in the quarter was 22.5%," while "Roto-Rooter gross margin of 51% was in line with our expectations" and the margin decline was "mainly caused by increased Internet marketing costs."

  • (CFO Witzeman) described water restoration execution issues: "In the first quarter, the average revenue per water restoration job declined by roughly 13%," and he said, "We anticipate that this issue will improve as the year progresses."

Q&A

  • Meghan Holtz, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: asked how much VITAS margin upside came from headcount and whether staffing would rise with higher ADC guidance; (CFO Witzeman) answered, "We averaged roughly 100 FTEs below our budget" and added, "We've increased [hiring]... to closer to 60 per month for the remainder of the year."

  • Meghan Holtz, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: asked about VITAS exposure to California hospice fraud enforcement; (President Wherley) responded, "We certainly are very sensitive to the national campaign to root out fraud, waste, and abuse," adding, "We are very supportive of the efforts" while also wanting to "avoid direct implications" that could "limit access for patients in need."

  • Meghan Holtz, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: asked if elevated Roto-Rooter marketing spend is the new run-rate; (CEO McNamara) said "to get that 3%, we had to battle... changes in the Google algorithm" and "we expect that to basically continue," while (CFO Witzeman) said guidance reflects "adding $1 million per quarter of marketing costs for the next 3 quarters."

  • Joanna Gajuk, BofA Securities, Research Division: asked why Roto-Rooter revenue growth guidance stayed the same despite acquisitions; (CFO Witzeman) cited offsets: "The contractor operations still performed slightly below our expectations" and "The water restoration revenue, particularly... price per job is still below... our expectations."

  • Joanna Gajuk, BofA Securities, Research Division: asked about collection improvements and the $4 million to $6 million annual expectation; (CFO Witzeman) said, "Yes, we were slightly better than that than our expectations in the first quarter" but noted, "part of that obviously comes from the idea that we're billing less per job."

  • Joanna Gajuk, BofA Securities, Research Division: asked about more franchise acquisitions; (CEO McNamara) said, "we're considering a number of possibilities" and "there's no question we anticipate continuing to add additional locations for Roto-Rooter," calling it "a good acquisition environment for us at this time."

  • Michael Murray, RBC Capital Markets, Research Division: asked how VITAS is balancing hospital vs. community admissions in new Florida markets; (President Wherley) said, "we're managing the balance" and "we feel really good about the balance," while (CFO Witzeman) added, "when we have a new start... they're all short-stay patients" and (CEO McNamara) said, "At least six months."

  • Michael Murray, RBC Capital Markets, Research Division: asked current paid-lead mix expectations; (CFO Witzeman) said, "Paid leads are roughly 53% to 54% of our total leads at the moment" and "We anticipate that mix to continue."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on the durability of VITAS margins (headcount), Roto-Rooter’s marketing run-rate, and whether acquisitions and collections changes meaningfully alter the 2026 outlook.

  • Management sentiment was slightly positive, anchored by confidence language around VITAS and normalization in Florida, including (CEO McNamara) saying, "We are more confident than ever" and (President Wherley) saying, "the opportunity for growth at VITAS has never been better," while being matter-of-fact on Roto-Rooter’s marketing pressure: (CEO McNamara) said, "it's going to be something approaching that" and (CFO Witzeman) said, "It's real early in Q2."

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing that Q4 "fell short of our expectations for both subsidiaries" (CEO McNamara, Q4 call), the current call’s tone shifted toward improved confidence, particularly through revised VITAS guidance and explicit discussion of franchise acquisitions and marketing-cost normalization.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Management’s headline shifted from Q4’s underperformance ("fell short of our expectations") to Q1’s VITAS upside ("exceeded even the high end of our expectations") and a guidance update that (CFO Witzeman) said was unusual: "Historically, we do not give quarterly updates to guidance."

  • VITAS narrative evolved from Q4’s deliberate hospital-admission tilt depressing profitability ("our revenue growth and EBITDA margin were lower than anticipated") to Q1 emphasizing mix rebalancing progress and added Florida cap cushion, with (CEO McNamara) saying Florida cap concerns are "behind us."

  • Roto-Rooter discussion remained centered on lead mix and marketing costs, but Q1 added weather-related disruption quantification and closed franchise purchases, with (CEO McNamara) calling San Francisco and Fort Worth "real plums" relative to typical availability.

  • Analyst questioning remained concentrated on Roto-Rooter’s structural pressures (marketing, SEO, collections, water restoration) and on VITAS regulatory scrutiny risk, expanding from Q4’s heavier focus on the Roto-Rooter turnaround credibility after the Q4 miss.

Risks and concerns

  • (CEO McNamara) said lead economics remain pressured by platform changes: "due to changes in the Google algorithm, our leads from the natural or free side... were down almost 16%" and "we've got to pay for more of the leads," with (CFO Witzeman) embedding higher spend by adding "$1 million per quarter of marketing costs" to guidance.

  • (CFO Witzeman) pointed to operational risk in water restoration billing transition: "we anticipated some disruption" and said revenue per job "declined by roughly 13%" during the shift to centralization.

  • (President Wherley) and (CEO McNamara) discussed heightened scrutiny around hospice fraud, with (CEO McNamara) warning, "we don't want to be a dolphin swept into a tuna net accidentally" as VITAS evaluates evolving CMS and state actions.

Final takeaway

Management portrayed Q1 as a pivot toward stronger confidence in 2026, led by VITAS outperformance, incremental Florida Medicare Cap cushion, and a formal raise to full-year consolidated adjusted EPS guidance to $24-$24.75 alongside higher VITAS ADC and revenue-growth assumptions. At Roto-Rooter, management kept revenue-growth expectations intact while acknowledging a more expensive lead environment tied to Google algorithm changes, modestly lower margin guidance to reflect sustained marketing costs, and ongoing execution work to stabilize water restoration billing and collections, while also stepping up franchise acquisitions in San Francisco and Fort Worth that were described as immediately accretive.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Semiconductor stocks lift Nasdaq this week as index hits record high
data.description:
The Nasdaq Composite Index (COMP:IND) hit a record high on Friday as the U.S. continued ceasefire negotiations with Iran. The index rose 1.6% to close at a record 24,836.60.

Semiconductor stocks rallied sharply on Friday, with Intel at the forefront, which soared as much as 24% after reporting blockbuster first-quarter results.

On a weekly basis, the tech-heavy index was pulled higher by chip stocks led by Arm Holdings (ARM), which logged the highest gains this week. Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) was second on the list of gainers this week.

Meanwhile, decliners were spread across a range of sectors, including communications, technology, and industrials.

Top gainers of the week:

Arm Holdings (ARM) +40.20%

Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) +24.57%

Texas Instruments (TXN) +20.49%

Intel (INTC) +18.93%

Marvell Technology (MRVL) +17.21%

Top losers for the week:

Charter Communications (CHTR) -24.66%

Cognizant Technology (CTSH) -10.15%

Honeywell (HON) -8.73%

CoStar (CSGP) -8.23%

GE HealthCare (GEHC) -7.85%

data.title: MetroCity Bankshares GAAP EPS of $0.77 beats by $0.05, revenue of $50.84M beats by $0.24M
data.description:
• MetroCity Bankshares press release (MCBS): Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.77 beats by $0.05.
  • Revenue of $50.84M (+41.2% Y/Y) beats by $0.24M.

data.title: No signs of cooling off in tech as Nasdaq ends at record powered by semis
data.description:
The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) surged to a record close on Friday, climbing +1.6% in a rally that Seeking Alpha analyst Alex King of Cestrian Capital Research said has been powered by an unstoppable semiconductor sector.

“This week’s market was brought to you by the letters Q, Q, and Q,” King wrote. “The mighty Nasdaq came to life, powered by semiconductors, which continue to head skywards. Thus far there is no sign of cooling off in tech, any attempts to fade the move since the March lows have just left investors battered, bruised, and resentful.”

The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) crossed the 10,000 mark this week, with the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) posting its 17th consecutive positive session. Also, Intel’s (INTC) first-quarter earnings beat, and upbeat guidance added fuel to the rally.

King now poses a provocative question: What if software joins the party? “The signs are there in IGV,” he said about the Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV). “We look forward to this most hated of moves becoming…even more hated.”

Michael Hartnett, chief investment strategist at BofA Global Research, warned the semiconductor index (SOX) appears “bubbly,” reaching overbought levels not seen since June 2000.

However, he noted the breakout among “blue collar” chip firms like ON Semiconductor (ON), STMicroelectronics (STM), and Texas Instruments (TXN) signals expectations for robust manufacturing expansion.

Michael Kantro of Piper Sandler pointed to broadening market participation, with more companies seeing positive earnings revisions alongside strong PMI readings.

Ryan Detrick of Carson Group added historical context: “Sell in May” periods tend to perform significantly better when stocks are already positive year-to-date, as they are now.

data.title: 3 things to look out for on Monday
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages ended mixed on Friday, but the S&P 500 (SP500) and the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) achieved another record close as investors digested the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension to the truce between Israel and Lebanon.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) ended +0.8% reaching the all-time high close of 7,165.08 points, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) closed +1.6%, also at the record close of 24,836.60 points. Meanwhile, the blue-chip Dow was -0.2%.

Now, here are three focus points for investors on Monday:

  • Notable companies due to report include Verizon (VZ), Public Storage (PSA), Nucor (NUE), and Domino's Pizza (DPZ). Options trading implies a double-digit swing for Rambus (RMBS) after it reports.
  • Pershing Square USA (PSUS) is expected to price its IPO and begin trading. Buyers of the new company will get exposure to Bill Ackman’s investing vehicle plus a small stake in the Pershing Square (PS) management company. The analyst quiet period expires on HMH Holding (HMH) to free up analysts to post ratings.
  • Monday has U.S. Treasury auctions for 3-month (US3M) bills, 6-month (US6M) bills, 2-year (US2Y) notes and 5-year (US5Y) notes, ahead of the Federal Reserve's rate decision later in the week.

data.title: Sensient targets high-single to double-digit 2026 local-currency growth while lifting Color Group outlook to double-digit growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sensient Technologies (SXT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Paul Manning said the company had “a very strong start to 2026,” citing “7% local currency revenue growth, 10% local currency adjusted EBITDA growth, and 14% local currency adjusted EPS growth,” and added that results “exceeded our early expectations and position us nicely for the year.”

  • Manning highlighted Color Group momentum and conversions: “Commercial activity around natural color conversions continues to be very strong and the momentum is building,” adding, “we are starting to see an uptick in customer orders for conversion of their synthetically colored products in the U.S., and the pipeline to $1 billion continues to look very promising.”

  • Manning framed strategy and capital focus around the U.S. shift to natural colors: “the preparations for the wholesale conversion of synthetic colors to natural colors in the United States remains our priority and current strategic focus,” and he reiterated that “the U.S. conversion to natural colors is the single largest opportunity in Sensient’s history.”

  • On geopolitics, Manning said, “we do not have any significant operations in the Middle East,” while adding the company is “working to mitigate any potential supply chain risks that may result from the overall increase in fuel and certain commodity prices,” and that with the war in Iran, “we have proven our ability to adjust prices where necessary.”

  • Tobin Tornehl said, “Sensient’s revenue was $435.8 million in the first quarter of 2026,” and added, “cash flow used in operations was $14 million in the first quarter of 2026,” with “capital expenditures…$29 million,” while noting “our net debt to credit adjusted EBITDA is 2.4x as of March 31, 2026.”

Outlook

  • Manning said, “we are increasing our local currency ranges for the year,” and the company “now expect[s] our local currency revenue to be up high-single to double-digits,” with “local currency adjusted EBITDA and EPS to grow at high-single to double-digit rates.”

  • Tornehl reiterated the cadence expectation: “We continue to expect acceleration in revenue and EBITDA growth in the second half of the year.”

  • Tornehl added near-term line items: “We expect our second quarter interest expense to be approximately $9 million, and we expect our second quarter adjusted tax rate to be approximately 25%,” and “we still expect the impact of currency on EPS to be immaterial for the year.”

  • On capital allocation, Manning said the company still expects “capital expenditures of $150 million to $170 million in 2026,” and “we do not anticipate any share buybacks at this time.”

Financial Results

  • Tornehl reported profitability and below-the-line items: “Operating income was $66.7 million in the first quarter of 2026,” “interest expense was $7.9 million,” and “the Company’s consolidated adjusted tax rate was 24.9% in the first quarter of 2026.”

  • Tornehl said, “Foreign currency translation had approximately a $0.06 benefit on EPS in the first quarter of 2026,” and also stated that “cash flow used in operations was $14 million,” alongside “capital expenditures…$29 million.”

  • On leverage and funding for the buildout, Tornehl said the company expects “higher investments in inventory throughout the year to prepare for increased natural Color Conversion revenue,” and that leverage is “expected to increase further with our leverage ratio entering the upper twos later in the year.”

Q&A

  • Ghansham Panjabi, Baird: asked what drove the better-than-expected quarter (wins vs. faster conversions vs. inventory load-in). Chairman, CEO, President Paul Manning: “the simple answer is we got more wins than we thought,” including “more natural color conversions than I had anticipated,” plus “we didn’t see as much of that tariff distortions that I was concerned about on the last call.”

  • Panjabi, Baird: asked about growth cadence through the year. CEO Manning: “Q2 will look pretty similar to Q1,” and “Q4 is where you’ll see perhaps a more decided inflection point in natural colors.”

  • Panjabi, Baird: asked whether the TiO2 opportunity is part of the $1 billion goal. CEO Manning: “titanium dioxide, from a regulatory standpoint, is actually considered natural colors,” but “I hadn’t contemplated that in the $1 billion.”

  • Joshua Spector, UBS: pressed on customer timeline slippage risks and confidence vs. 3 months ago. CEO Manning: “I’m very exceedingly confident,” adding, “there is no slowdown at any of these customers,” and pointing to “January 1, 2027…[and] January 1, 2028” as key target dates.

  • Spector, UBS: asked about Color margins and OpEx investment levels. CEO Manning: “net-net for the year, we will be flattish on the EBITDA in the Color Group,” explaining the balance between depreciation timing, staffing, and R&D/application work.

  • Lawrence Solow, CJS: asked if better Color revenue timing explains improved margin expectations with no change in investments. CEO Manning: “revenue was a little bit better than we thought,” and “we did better than we thought…so therefore, the EBITDA was not down as I had thought it might be.”

  • Solow, CJS: asked about “musical chairs” supply-chain readiness ahead of 2027–2028. CEO Manning: “the bulk of the activity is going to be in 2027,” while warning of a possible “tipping point” and saying, “we’re hitting it hard…on capital…[and] the supply chain.”

  • Solow, CJS: asked about FDA involvement in natural colors approvals. CEO Manning: “Colors actually have to go through a…approval process with the FDA,” and “there’s a lot more…petitioning to use a new natural color in the market.”

  • Ming Tang, BNP Paribas: asked for an update on conversion revenue (previously about $5 million). CEO Manning: “when you take that back-half and you take Q1 of this year, now we’ve invoiced about $20 million or so towards that goal of natural colors.”

  • Tang, BNP Paribas: challenged the EPS guidance math vs. Q1 dynamics and risks. CEO Manning: “as you get below EBITDA…you then start to have to factor in things like interest and tax,” and CFO Tornehl added, “we expect our overall interest expense to be up about $6 million throughout this year,” while noting Q1 FX benefited EPS but “in the back half of the year, that would become more of a headwind.”

  • Tang, BNP Paribas: asked about raw-material inflation and disruption exposure. CEO Manning: “we believe that there is a sufficient amount of inflationary inputs that we’re going to need to take pricing to address that…low-single-digit magnitude,” and said pressures include “logistical inflation,” packaging, and “synthetic colors…derived” from petroleum-based inputs.

  • Spector, UBS (follow-up): asked if Q2 guidance bakes in logistics cost headwinds and price offsets. CEO Manning: “I’m not assuming any bad, and I’m not assuming any good,” and “I’m also not assuming any pricing in Q2.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative/pressing around timing, margins, and guidance math, including Spector’s “a lot of investors are concerned that things could slip” and Tang’s question about an “implied downgrade on the rest of the year.”

  • Management tone was positive/confident in prepared remarks and firm in Q&A, with Manning saying, “I’m very exceedingly confident,” and “there is no slowdown at any of these customers,” while acknowledging variability in “the precise distribution” of monthly conversions.

  • Versus last quarter, management language shifted from expecting investment-driven margin pressure to indicating Color EBITDA margin should be “flattish” for the year, while analyst questioning remained focused on the conversion timeline and profit leverage.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was raised: in Q1 2026, Manning said full-year local-currency revenue is now expected “up high-single to double-digits,” versus Q4 2025 guidance of “mid-single digit to double digits,” and Q1 2026 raised expected local-currency adjusted EPS growth to “high-single to double-digit” from the prior “mid-single to high-single-digit.”

  • Color Group outlook tightened upward: in Q1 2026, Manning said, “I now expect the Color Group to deliver double-digit local currency revenue growth in 2026,” versus Q4 2025’s “high single-digit to double-digit range.”

  • On tariffs and Asia Pacific, Q1 2026 commentary indicated less disruption than feared (“we didn’t see as much of that tariff distortions”), compared with Q4 2025 where tariff activity and supply-chain disruptions were described as weighing on Asia Pacific results and early-2026 expectations.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged potential cost and supply-chain impacts tied to geopolitics: Manning referenced “trade, tariffs, and geopolitics,” and said the company is mitigating possible impacts from “overall increase in fuel and certain commodity prices.”

  • Inflation and logistics were identified as likely requiring pricing actions: Manning said inflationary inputs may require “a modest amount of price increase,” spanning logistics and certain synthetic and natural inputs.

  • Financing and FX were cited as swing factors for EPS: Tornehl said interest expense is expected to rise with investment and inventory builds, and management said FX could be “more of a headwind” in the back half even if “immaterial for the year” overall.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as an early upside print driven by “more wins than we thought,” and paired it with higher full-year growth ranges and a more explicit expectation for “double-digit” Color Group growth in 2026, while emphasizing that increased investment and a rising interest burden remain part of the plan to prepare capacity, supply chain, and technical resources for the U.S. natural color conversion opportunity tied to customers’ 2027–2028 timelines and Sensient’s $1 billion natural color sales goal.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Heidmar Maritime Holdings faces Nasdaq $1 compliance rule breach
data.description:
• Heidmar Maritime (HMR) announced on Friday that it received a notice from Nasdaq saying its stock price has stayed below the required $1 minimum for 30 straight trading days, so it is currently not compliant with listing rules.
  • The company now has a 180-day grace period (until Oct. 19, 2026) to resolve this issue and get back in compliance.
  • To regain compliance, its stock needs to close at $1 or above for at least 10 consecutive trading days.
  • Until then, its business operations remain unaffected, and the stock continues trading on Nasdaq.
  • The stock is trading higher at ~$0.82.
  • Source: Press Release
